summary: DHH argues that Silicon Valley's toxic startup culture stems from founders and employees who plan short tenures followed by big exits. When you don't plan to stick around, it's easy to mistreat employees, customers, and society without regret. He contrasts this with Basecamp's long-term mindset, where decisions are made with decades of consequences in mind. The post frames permanence as a moral incentive that naturally produces better behavior.
key_points: The 'loot and leave' mentality explains why startup culture tolerates harmful behavior — short tenures make villainy bearable
Planning to stay for decades fundamentally changes how you make decisions about employees, customers, and technology
Permanence functions as an intrinsic moral incentive, similar to how people treat their own neighborhood better than one they're visiting
The worst startup behaviors are concentrated below the titan level, where there's no empire to justify the means
money_quotes: If you don't plan to stick around, who cares how you leave the things behind?
Employees, customers, regulations, and, hell, even society at large, is much easier to screw over without regret if you don't have to stick around for all that long.
A few years of being the villain or the asshole is probably something a lot more people can imagine tolerating than if it was the condemnation of a whole career.
If I'm going to be here for the next 10–20–30 years, what's the right move that I won't regret over the coming decades?
That perspective of permanence gives you a completely different outlook on your actions and your overall strategy.
It's like how most people end up treating a neighborhood they live in with a different kind of respect than one they're just visiting.
How would things be different for you if you couldn't just loot'n'leave?
key_insight: The short-term exit mentality of startup culture removes the natural moral incentive of permanence, making it easy to exploit people and cut corners when you won't be around to face the consequences.
